data:
Search results for "llvmlite Python IRBuilder tutorial example build LLVM IR":

Title: Building and using llvmlite - a basic example - Eli Bendersky's website
URL: https://eli.thegreenplace.net/2015/building-and-using-llvmlite-a-basic-example
Content: llvmlite was created last year by the developers of Numba (a JIT compiler for scientific Python), and just recently replaced llvmpy as their bridge to LLVM. One of the reasons the Numba devs decided to ditch llvmpy in favor of a new approach is the biggest issue heavy users of LLVM as a library have - its incredible rate of API-breaking change. The LLVM C++ API is notoriously unstable and will remain this way for the foreseeable future. Since building the IR using repeated FFI calls to LLVM proved to be slow and error-prone in llvmpy, llvmlite re-implemented the IR builder in pure Python. This also reduces the "API surface" llvmlite uses, since the textual representation of LLVM IR is one of the more stable things in LLVM. LLVM has a great tool named llvm-config in its binary image, and the Makefile in llvmlite uses it, which means building llvmlite with any version of LLVM I want is just a simple matter of running:.

Title: IR builders — llvmlite 0.48.0dev0+54.ge8d38ef.dirty documentation
URL: https://llvmlite.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user-guide/ir/ir-builder.html
Content: It allows you to fill the basic blocks of your functions with LLVM instructions. An `IRBuilder` internally maintains a current basic block and a pointer inside the block’s list of instructions. When a new instruction is added, it is inserted at that point, and then the pointer is advanced after the new instruction. A `IRBuilder` also maintains a reference to metadata describing the current source location, which is attached to all inserted instructions. If *block*—a `Block`—is given, the builder starts at the end of this basic block. :   The basic block that the builder is operating on. :   The function that the builder is operating on. :   Append a basic block, with the given optional *name*, to the current function. The current block is also changed to the instruction’s basic block. :   Position at the end of the basic *block*. :   Position the builder either at the end of the basic *block*, if it is not terminated, or just before the *block*’s terminator:.

Title: python - Link C in llvmlite
URL: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/36658726/link-c-in-llvmlite
Content: 1. Stack Internal Implement a knowledge platform layer to power your enterprise and AI tools. 3. Stack Ads Connect your brand to the world’s most trusted technologist communities. 4. Releases Keep up-to-date on features we add to Stack Overflow and Stack Internal. For this, i've already implemented some functions in C, and now i want to call these functions using `builder.call` from llvmlite. I have not found documentation or examples of how to do this. I could write the functions directly using llvmlite builders, but will be much quick, cleaner and easy do it in C. Do you have an example of how to perform the call? And if so, how do I then find the function to call it? On the LLVM IR side you can just declare the functions with the right signature (and no body), and insert calls to them like any other function. This is just like how in C you might call a function which is defined in another file.

Title: llvmlite · PyPI
URL: https://pypi.org/project/llvmlite/0.4.0
Content: # llvmlite. A lightweight LLVM python binding for writing JIT compilers. Old llvmpy binding exposes a lot of LLVM but the mapping of C++ style memory management to python is error prone. Numba and many JIT compiler does not need a full LLVM API. llvmlite is a project originally tailored for Numba's needs, using the following approach:. - A small C wrapper around the parts of the LLVM C++ API we need that are. - A ctypes Python wrapper around the C API. - A pure Python implementation of the subset of the LLVM IR builder that we. - llvmlite IR builder is pure python. - Most of llvmlite uses the LLVM C API which is small but very stable and backward compatible (low maintenance when changing LLVM version). - The binding is not a python C-extension (no need to wrestle with Python's compiler requirement and C++11 compatibility). Details for the file `llvmlite-0.4.0.tar.gz`.

Title: LLVMlite Documentation — COS 320 2024 - cs.Princeton
URL: https://www.cs.princeton.edu/courses/archive/spring24/cos320/hw/hw2/llvmlite.html
Content: LLVMlite is a small subset of the LLVM IR that we will be using throughout the course as the intermediate representation in our compiler. This document explains the structure of well-formed LLVMlite programs, the semantics of LLVMlite in terms of an abstract machine, and the relevant parts of the code provided with the assignments. To give you a sense of structure of LLVMlite programs and the most basic features, the following is our running example, the simple recursive factorial function written in the concrete syntax of the LLVMlite IR. The argument is prefixed with `%` to indicate that it’s an identifier local to the function, while `fac` is prefixed with `@` to indicate that it is global (i.e. in scope in the entire compilation unit). Next, at (2) we have the first instruction of the body of `fac`, which performs a signed comparison of the argument `%n` and 0 and assigns the result to the temporary `%1`.
